lesson.title: Counting & Combinatorics
lesson.body: A permutation is an ordered arrangement: P(n,r) = n!/(n−r)!. A combination is an unordered selection: C(n,r) = n!/(r!(n−r)!). The multiplication rule: if event A has m outcomes and B has n outcomes, together they have m×n outcomes.

rooms.question: A restaurant has 3 starters and 4 mains. How many 1-starter+1-main meals?
steps: \text{Each starter pairs with each main independently}
rooms.explanation: The multiplication rule: when two independent choices are made, multiply the options. 3 starters × 4 mains = 12 combinations. Adding (3+4=7) would be wrong — that counts choosing one or the other, not both.

rooms.question: How many ways can you arrange 3 distinct books on a shelf?
rooms.explanation: For n distinct objects, there are n! arrangements. 3! = 3×2×1 = 6. Think of it: 3 choices for the first spot, then 2 remain, then 1. Multiplying gives 6.

rooms.question: How many 2-letter codes can be made from {A, B, C} without repeating?
rooms.explanation: P(n,r) = n!/(n−r)! counts ordered arrangements of r items from n. P(3,2) = 3!/1! = 6. Order matters: AB and BA are different codes. The 6 codes are: AB, AC, BA, BC, CA, CB.

rooms.question: How many ways to choose 2 items from 4 (order does not matter)?
rooms.explanation: C(4,2) = 4!/(2!×2!) = 6. Unlike permutations, order doesn't matter: {A,B} = {B,A}. We divide P(4,2)=12 by 2! because each pair is counted twice in the ordered version.

rooms.question: Order the steps to compute C(5,2):
rooms.explanation: The combination formula C(n,r) = n!/(r!×(n−r)!). For C(5,2): write the formula, compute 5!=120, compute the denominator factorials (2!=2, 3!=6), then divide: 120/12=10.

questions.question: A coin is flipped 4 times. How many total outcomes?
questions.explanation: Each flip has 2 outcomes (H or T), and the flips are independent. By the multiplication rule: 2×2×2×2 = 2⁴ = 16 total sequences (HHHH, HHHT, ..., TTTT).

questions.question: P(6,3) = ?
questions.explanation: P(6,3) = 6!/(6−3)! = 6!/3! = (6×5×4×3!)/(3!) = 6×5×4 = 120. The 3! in numerator and denominator cancel, leaving just the top 3 factors of 6!.

questions.question: How many 3-person committees from 5 people?
questions.explanation: C(5,3) = 5!/(3!×2!) = 120/(6×2) = 10. A committee has no ordering, so we use combinations not permutations. Note C(5,3) = C(5,2) = 10 — choosing 3 to include is the same as choosing 2 to exclude.
